title: Are You the One?
subtitle: John, bound in prison, sends his disciples to ask the Lord — and Christ answers in deeds

stillness: The body is a prison, hindering us from the contemplation of truth.
Be still, then, and let grace not be held bound.
Come away into the desert of repentance.
gospel: Now John the Baptist, set fast in chains and in prison,
had heard through his disciples of the works of Christ,
and of the miracles that he was doing.
And John said to him — that is, to Christ —
"Are you he who is to come,
the Christ and Messiah promised in the Law,
or do we look for another to save us?"
And Jesus, answering in word and in deed, said to them:
"Go, and tell John what you have heard and seen:
the blind see, the lame walk,
the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear,
the poor have the gospel preached to them."
behold: John is in chains; grace itself is held bound.
Alas, in how many today is John held in chains!
For the body is a prison, hindering a man from the contemplation of truth.
Yet John himself did not doubt;
he who leapt for joy at Christ's presence in his mother's womb did not doubt.
He who said, "I ought to be baptized by you, and do you come to me?"
He who, at the Baptism, knew him by the dove descending,
and by the voice and testimony of the Father,
and afterward pointed him out with his finger —
he, set in prison for Christ and for his faith and justice,
took on the manner of one who doubts, and asked.
Knowing himself already appointed for departure,
he wished to join his disciples to Christ —
as a provident father, dying, entrusts his sons to a faithful guardian.
He longed, while still living, to see the full faith of his disciples,
and to see them believing Christ without any doubt.
consider: Jesus loved the doubting of the disciples,
and showed them first by deeds, and afterward by words, that he was the Christ.
In their presence he cured the blind, the deaf, the lame, and the lepers,
and then preached to the people, whose Gospel the poor first received;
for the poor and humble believe the Gospel more readily than the rich.
"You see me — then know me; you see the deeds — then know the doer.
I give light to the blind, I raise the dead, I convert the poor to faith.
The very works that I do bear witness of me;
if you will not believe me, believe the works."
He did not wish to say in open words, "I am he," to give an example of avoiding arrogance,
yet most plainly he showed himself to be the one of whom they asked.
For the testimony that is from deeds is more believable and more sound than the testimony from words;
and the voice of deed is mightier than the voice of speech.
Better in God's sight is one just man than the whole world full of sinners.
prayer: since you are he who is to come to save us,
and we look for no other:
grant that, bound by your love and fear,
we may come out of the reed-bed of vanity
and out of the soft clothing of pleasure,
Declare to us, by the working of your mercy,
that you are the truth, by which the inwardly blind see;
the charity, by which the lame walk;
the humility, by which the proud lepers are cleansed;
the word, by which the deaf hear;
the life, by which the dead rise again;
and the power, by which the poor have the gospel preached to them —
that all may be converted to you. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Videtis me, agnoscite me; videtis facta, agnoscite factorem.
latinJewel.english: You see me — then know me; you see the deeds — then know the doer.
